# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, task-distribution details, evaluation setup details, and reported performance findings about agentic backend coding across full development lifecycles.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: claim-support
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ABC-Bench is introduced as a benchmark for agentic backend coding in a realistic executable workflow.

## Source Excerpt

The abstract states that the authors "introduce ABC-Bench, a benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate agentic back-end coding within a realistic executable workflow."

## Note

This is a benchmark-description claim from the paper, not an external validation of benchmark quality.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The benchmark contains 224 curated tasks spanning 8 programming languages and 19 frameworks.

## Source Excerpt

The abstract says the authors "curated 224 practical tasks spanning 8 languages and 19 frameworks from open-source repositories." Later sections repeat that the final benchmark contains "224 high-quality tasks spanning 8 programming languages and 19 web frameworks."

## Note

The source presents these counts as final dataset composition.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ABC-Bench requires agents to complete the full backend lifecycle from repository exploration through deployment and external API-level validation.

## Source Excerpt

The paper says ABC-Bench "requires the agents to manage the entire development lifecycle from repository exploration to instantiating containerized services and pass the external end-to-end API tests." Table 1 further lists five stages: repository exploration, code editing, environment setup, deployment, and end-to-end testing.

## Note

This directly specifies what the benchmark operationally tests.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: claim-support
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper claims ABC-Bench is the only benchmark in its comparison that covers the full five-stage backend development lifecycle.

## Source Excerpt

In the benchmark-scope comparison, the paper says: "ABC-Bench is the only benchmark to encompass this entire lifecycle," referring to repository exploration, code editing, environment setup, deployment, and end-to-end testing.

## Note

This is a comparative claim made by the authors within their benchmark comparison table.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The task-generation pipeline initially produced 600 candidate tasks, which were filtered down to 224 final tasks; 92 of the final tasks additionally require autonomous environment configuration.

## Source Excerpt

Section 4.3 says: "Through this pipeline, we initially generated 600 candidate tasks... resulting in a final set of 224 high-quality tasks... of which 92 additionally require autonomous environment configuration."

## Note

This is a dataset-construction count, not a model-performance result.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Even the top-performing model reported in the paper achieves only a 63.2% Pass@1 score on the benchmark.

## Source Excerpt

The introduction states: "Even the top-performing model achieves only a 63.2% Pass@1 rate," and the main-results section reports Claude Sonnet 4.5 as the strongest model with overall Pass@1 around 63.2%.

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-relevant only in a bounded way because it shows a performance ceiling without itself explaining why the ceiling occurs. It carries tension because it contrasts current model performance with the benchmark's stated practical demands.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper identifies environment configuration and deployment as persistent or predominant bottlenecks in benchmark performance.

## Source Excerpt

The introduction says "environment configuration and deployment" are "persistent bottlenecks, often acting as the primary barriers to success." The contributions section similarly says the evaluation uncovers that "environment configuration and deployment are the predominant bottlenecks."

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it locates the main failure region but does not fully specify causal pathways for each failure. It introduces tension because stronger functional coding ability does not eliminate these setup bottlenecks.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Performance varies strongly by language stack, and the paper reports that most models score 0.0% on Rust tasks.

## Source Excerpt

The main-results section says performance "varies significantly by language stack" and describes Rust as "an extreme case where most models... fail to solve a single task, scoring 0.0%." It adds that only the most capable proprietary models, specifically Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT-5, achieve meaningful success above 30%.

## Note

Confidence is medium because the captured markdown is noisy, but the Rust failure pattern is stated repeatedly and with explicit percentages. This is bounded mechanism evidence because it shows where capability breaks without fully explaining why. It is tension-relevant because it shows large cross-stack weakness despite stronger overall results elsewhere.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: incident
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Smaller models are reported to fail disproportionately on basic path-missing errors, with Qwen3-8B showing 76 such instances versus 19 for GPT-5.

## Source Excerpt

In the error-analysis section, the paper says environment configuration remains a universal bottleneck and notes that the smaller Qwen3-8B model suffers disproportionately from "fundamental Path Missing errors (76 instances), nearly four times that of GPT-5 (19 instances)."

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it narrows a concrete failure mode without fully resolving the deeper cause. It is tension-relevant because it shows lower-level filesystem/navigation failures persisting within coding-agent evaluations.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-011
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Yang et al., ABC-Bench, arXiv:2601.11077v1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.11077
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper reports a strong positive correlation between agent interaction depth and task success, with r = 0.87.

## Source Excerpt

The analysis section says there is a "strong positive correlation (r = 0.87) between the depth of agent interaction and task success," and Figure 5 is described as showing this relationship between average agent turns and Average Pass@1.

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it links longer interaction trajectories to higher success but does not establish that longer interaction itself causes better outcomes.
---

ABC-Bench requires agents to complete the full backend lifecycle from repository exploration through deployment and external API-level validation.

## Source Excerpt

The paper says ABC-Bench "requires the agents to manage the entire development lifecycle from repository exploration to instantiating containerized services and pass the external end-to-end API tests." Table 1 further lists five stages: repository exploration, code editing, environment setup, deployment, and end-to-end testing.

## Note

This directly specifies what the benchmark operationally tests.

Even the top-performing model reported in the paper achieves only a 63.2% Pass@1 score on the benchmark.

## Source Excerpt

The introduction states: "Even the top-performing model achieves only a 63.2% Pass@1 rate," and the main-results section reports Claude Sonnet 4.5 as the strongest model with overall Pass@1 around 63.2%.

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-relevant only in a bounded way because it shows a performance ceiling without itself explaining why the ceiling occurs. It carries tension because it contrasts current model performance with the benchmark's stated practical demands.
```

The paper identifies environment configuration and deployment as persistent or predominant bottlenecks in benchmark performance.

## Source Excerpt

The introduction says "environment configuration and deployment" are "persistent bottlenecks, often acting as the primary barriers to success." The contributions section similarly says the evaluation uncovers that "environment configuration and deployment are the predominant bottlenecks."

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it locates the main failure region but does not fully specify causal pathways for each failure. It introduces tension because stronger functional coding ability does not eliminate these setup bottlenecks.
```

Performance varies strongly by language stack, and the paper reports that most models score 0.0% on Rust tasks.

## Source Excerpt

The main-results section says performance "varies significantly by language stack" and describes Rust as "an extreme case where most models... fail to solve a single task, scoring 0.0%." It adds that only the most capable proprietary models, specifically Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT-5, achieve meaningful success above 30%.

## Note

Confidence is medium because the captured markdown is noisy, but the Rust failure pattern is stated repeatedly and with explicit percentages. This is bounded mechanism evidence because it shows where capability breaks without fully explaining why. It is tension-relevant because it shows large cross-stack weakness despite stronger overall results elsewhere.
```

Smaller models are reported to fail disproportionately on basic path-missing errors, with Qwen3-8B showing 76 such instances versus 19 for GPT-5.

## Source Excerpt

In the error-analysis section, the paper says environment configuration remains a universal bottleneck and notes that the smaller Qwen3-8B model suffers disproportionately from "fundamental Path Missing errors (76 instances), nearly four times that of GPT-5 (19 instances)."

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it narrows a concrete failure mode without fully resolving the deeper cause. It is tension-relevant because it shows lower-level filesystem/navigation failures persisting within coding-agent evaluations.
```

The paper reports a strong positive correlation between agent interaction depth and task success, with r = 0.87.

## Source Excerpt

The analysis section says there is a "strong positive correlation (r = 0.87) between the depth of agent interaction and task success," and Figure 5 is described as showing this relationship between average agent turns and Average Pass@1.

## Note

This is bounded mechanism evidence because it links longer interaction trajectories to higher success but does not establish that longer interaction itself causes better outcomes.
```
